history: 53-year-old white male with a history of HCV/EtOH cirrhosis (on Harvoni), COPD with ongoing tobacco use, IDDM, and jugular vein thrombosis (on warfarin), presented to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain and dyspnea. The patient was referred from a liver clinic due to a 3-day history of intermittent, productive cough with scant yellow sputum and sharp, central chest pain that occurs with coughing. The patient also reported fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing. There were no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, upper respiratory symptoms, subjective fevers, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, rashes, melena, hematochezia, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), or edema. The patient walked into the ED and has allergies to codeine and penicillins. BP 119/71, P 91, RR 22, SpO2 97%, T 98.9掳 F, Pain 8.

history: 64-year-old white male with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolemia, depression, anxiety, and chronic neck and low back pain. Recently underwent C5 corpectomy and C6-7 ACDF. He presents with difficulty swallowing, increased swelling around the surgical incision, and hoarseness. Unable to swallow pills or water, regurgitating attempts. Reports no stridor or breathing difficulties. Recently used cocaine, contributing to hypertensive urgency with BP at 180/104, headache, and chest pain. Arrived by ambulance, allergic to Tegretol.

history: A 49-year-old male with a significant medical history including end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and recent hospitalization for wet gangrene and osteomyelitis, presents with fever and signs of sepsis, including hypotension and tachycardia. The patient also exhibits intermittent agitation and anxiety. He is allergic to dobutamine.

history: A 49-year-old African American male presents to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain. He has a significant medical history, including paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (currently not on anticoagulation), cervical cord compression with associated complications, and a history of substance use (tobacco, alcohol, and IVDU on methadone). The patient describes his chest pain as sub-sternal, radiating to the left arm and jaw, with associated diaphoresis, shortness of breath, and palpitations. The pain occurs at rest, and he reports worsening orthopnea over the past two months. Vital signs are stable, and he has no known drug allergies. The patient walked into the ED and is currently not on anticoagulation despite atrial fibrillation.

history: A 78-year-old white female with a complex medical history including hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, chronic kidney disease stage 3, polymyalgia rheumatica, hyperlipidemia, anemia, hyperparathyroidism, and recent metastatic malignancy status post resection of a cerebellar mass presents to the emergency department with fever, presumed pneumonia, and shock. She arrives via ambulance. The patient has experienced severe hypotension and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). She was recently intubated for airway protection and had a central line placed for vascular access. Her vital signs on arrival were BP 120/58, HR 150, RR 20, SpO2 97%, and temperature 98.8掳 F, with no reported pain. The patient has a known allergy to penicillins.

history: A 72-year-old female with a complex medical history including heart failure, aortic stenosis, history of strokes, COPD, and chronic urinary retention with a Foley catheter, presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath, chest pain, and bilateral pedal edema. The patient reports a one-week history of worsening shortness of breath and pedal edema, along with intermittent left-sided chest and arm pain. On the day of admission, she experienced worsening shortness of breath, requiring oxygen supplementation. The patient denies cough, fever, abdominal pain, or recent weight gain. She was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies.

history: A 41-year-old white male with a complex medical history including HIV, diabetes mellitus type 2, hidradenitis suppurativa, chronic renal insufficiency, hyperlipidemia, asthma, obstructive sleep apnea, and obesity, presents with a chief complaint of perianal abscess. The patient experiences significant pain exacerbated by sitting, similar to previous abscess episodes, but denies fever, chills, drainage, diarrhea, or bloody stools. He has a history of internal anal condylomata excision and appendectomy. The patient is allergic to erythromycin base. He walked into the ED with vital signs: HR 98, RR 18, BP 126/71, SpO2 100%, Temp 97.8掳F, and pain rated 8.

history: A 62-year-old white male with a history of esophageal cancer (status post-esophagectomy), right-sided CVA, chronic pain syndrome on chronic opioids, COPD, and depression presents to the ED with altered mental status. The patient's wife found him having a bizarre conversation, not oriented to date or time, and he had fallen without head trauma. Suspected factors for AMS include possible narcotic overdose (pinpoint pupils, slurred speech). The patient has chronic left-sided weakness post-CVA and was found with a pill bottle, suggesting an extra dose of oxycontin may have been taken. The patient was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies. He is alert to person and place but complains of significant thirst.
outcome: A 62-year-old white male with a history of esophageal cancer (status post-esophagectomy), right-sided CVA, chronic pain syndrome on chronic opioids, COPD, and depression presents to the ED with altered mental status. history: A 51-year-old Asian male with a past medical history of ESRD secondary to IgA nephropathy, hypertension, adrenal insufficiency, and other comorbidities presented to the ED with nausea, vomiting, and left-sided weakness. The patient was noted to be flaccid on the left side with possible gaze deviation and actively vomiting. Vital signs on arrival were BP 198/107, P 108, RR 22, SpO2 97%, and T 97.0掳 F. He was transported to the ED by ambulance and has an allergy to isoniazid.
outcome: A 51-year-old Asian male with a past medical history of ESRD secondary to IgA nephropathy, hypertension, adrenal insufficiency, and other comorbidities presented to the ED with nausea, vomiting, and left-sided weakness. augmentation.likely_working_diagnosis: Right wrist pain, likely sprain or contusion pending radiograph

ddx.support: Walk-in presentation, stable vital signs, isolated wrist pain, and home disposition support a lower-acuity musculoskeletal injury.
ddx.against_or_missing: The source does not document mechanism, swelling, deformity, or range of motion.
ddx.next_discriminator: Focused wrist exam and plain radiographs if point tenderness or functional limitation is present.
ddx.acuity_implication: ESI 4 is consistent with one expected resource when no neurovascular compromise is present.

ddx.diagnosis: Distal radius, scaphoid, or carpal fracture
ddx.support: Pain 8/10 and recorded exam or imaging resource make fracture a necessary consideration.
ddx.against_or_missing: No documented deformity, open wound, or neurovascular deficit in the source bundle.
ddx.next_discriminator: Point tenderness, snuffbox tenderness, swelling, grip strength, and x-ray result.
ddx.acuity_implication: Escalates if deformity, open injury, or neurovascular compromise is found.

teaching_points: For isolated extremity pain, document deformity, swelling, range of motion, distal pulse, sensation, motor function, and weight-bearing or functional status.
Severe pain changes reassessment needs even when ESI remains low acuity.

inferred_facts.statement: Focused exam should assess right wrist swelling, deformity, focal bony tenderness, range of motion, grip strength, radial pulse, capillary refill, and sensation.
inferred_facts.rationale: The source documents isolated right wrist pain with pain 8/10 and one exam or imaging resource, but no structured extremity exam findings.
inferred_facts.expected_action: Perform and document focused wrist exam before deciding whether imaging, splinting, analgesia, or outpatient follow-up is sufficient.
inferred_facts.practice_rule: For wrist pain, connect pain severity and exam findings to imaging need, immobilization, and reassessment.

augmentation.likely_working_diagnosis: Right foot swelling and pain requiring evaluation for injury, infection, crystal arthritis, or vascular cause

ddx.diagnosis: Soft tissue injury, occult fracture, or inflammatory arthritis
ddx.support: Right foot swelling, pain 7/10, exam or imaging resource, and home disposition fit a stable but resource-requiring extremity presentation.
ddx.against_or_missing: The source does not document trauma, focal tenderness, erythema, warmth, wound, or ability to bear weight.
ddx.next_discriminator: Focused foot exam, weight-bearing status, skin findings, and radiograph if trauma or focal bony tenderness is present.
ddx.acuity_implication: ESI 3 is supported by multiple resources and pain requiring treatment and reassessment.

ddx.diagnosis: Cellulitis, abscess, gout, or septic arthritis
ddx.support: Swelling with pain can reflect infection or crystal arthritis, especially when laboratory testing is recorded.
ddx.against_or_missing: No fever, wound, erythema, warmth, immunosuppression, or joint range limitation is documented in the source bundle.
ddx.next_discriminator: Inspect skin, assess joint range of motion, check fever/systemic symptoms, and use labs or imaging if infection is plausible.
ddx.acuity_implication: Escalates if systemic illness, rapidly spreading erythema, severe joint pain with limited range of motion, or vascular compromise is found.

teaching_points: Foot swelling is not a diagnosis. The learner should separate trauma, infection, inflammatory arthritis, and vascular causes with focused exam questions.
Multiple recorded resources make ESI 3 more plausible than a one-resource extremity complaint.

inferred_facts.statement: Focused exam should document right foot location of swelling, erythema or warmth, wounds, focal bony tenderness, range of motion, weight-bearing ability, dorsalis pedis pulse, capillary refill, motor function, and sensation.
inferred_facts.rationale: The source documents right foot swelling, pain 7/10, hypertension, laboratory testing, and one exam or imaging resource, but no physical exam details.
inferred_facts.expected_action: Treat pain, perform focused foot and neurovascular exam, and use labs or imaging to distinguish injury, infection, crystal arthritis, or vascular compromise.
inferred_facts.practice_rule: For foot swelling, name the exam finding that separates injury, infection, inflammatory arthritis, and vascular risk.

history: A 67-year-old Black/African American male with a history of hypertension, bilateral pulmonary emboli, stage II chronic kidney disease, and metastatic renal cell carcinoma to the lungs and mediastinum, presented to the ED with abdominal pain, vomiting, nausea, and dyspnea. He recently underwent a right radical nephrectomy one month ago and has been experiencing right lower quadrant pain since the surgery, with non-bilious non-bloody emesis in the past two weeks. This morning, he developed acute right-sided chest pain and dyspnea that resolved with rest. He denies fever but reports taking oxycodone for post-surgical abdominal pain. The patient is a walk-in arrival with no known allergies. Vital signs: BP 166/84, HR 85, RR 16, SpO2 90%, T 99.6掳 F. Pain is rated at 7/10. Family history includes multiple early deaths from coronary artery disease among siblings and renal cancer in the mother.

history: A 40-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism and depression, allergic to erythromycin base, was transferred to the emergency department with a chief complaint of left leg pain. The patient sustained injuries after falling from a porch, resulting in an open left tibia/fibula fracture, a nondisplaced left medial malleolar fracture, and a fracture of the base of the left metatarsal. She reported falling after the porch railing gave way, landing on her left leg. Her vital signs upon arrival were stable, with a heart rate of 100, respiratory rate of 16, blood pressure of 138/78, SpO2 at 98%, and temperature of 98.1掳F. The patient rated her pain at 2/10.
outcome: A 40-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism and depression, allergic to erythromycin base, was transferred to the emergency department with a chief complaint of left leg pain. documented_evidence.statement: Chief complaint: L Leg injury, Transfer.

A 40-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism and depression, allergic to erythromycin base, was transferred to the emergency department with a chief complaint of left leg pain. The patient sustained injuries after falling from a porch, resulting in an open left tibia/fibula fracture, a nondisplaced left medial malleolar fracture, and a fracture of the base of the left metatarsal. She reported falling after the porch railing gave way, landing on her left leg. Her vital signs upon arrival were stable, with a heart rate of 100, respiratory rate of 16, blood pressure of 138/78, SpO2 at 98%, and temperature of 98.1掳F. The patient rated her pain at 2/10.
Temperature 98.1 F, heart rate 100.0, respiratory rate 16.0, oxygen saturation 98.0%, blood pressure 138/78, pain 2.0/10.
documented_evidence.source_field: temperature, heartrate, resprate, o2sat, sbp, dbp, pain

augmentation.likely_working_diagnosis: Open left tibia/fibula fracture with associated ankle and foot fractures after fall

ddx.diagnosis: Open tibia/fibula fracture with associated fractures
ddx.support: Transfer note documents open tibia/fibula fracture, medial malleolar fracture, metatarsal fracture, fall mechanism, admission, and high resource use.
ddx.against_or_missing: The current source bundle does not provide distal neurovascular exam or compartment findings.
ddx.next_discriminator: Distal pulses, sensation, motor function, compartment exam, wound contamination, antibiotic timing, and orthopedic plan.
ddx.acuity_implication: Reference ESI 2 is supported by open fracture, transfer, admission, and multiple expected resources.

ddx.diagnosis: Neurovascular compromise or evolving compartment syndrome
ddx.support: Open long-bone fracture requires serial neurovascular and compartment assessment even when pain score is low after transfer treatment.
ddx.against_or_missing: No pulse deficit, sensory deficit, motor deficit, or tense compartments are documented in the source bundle.
ddx.next_discriminator: Serial pain, passive stretch pain, compartments, pulses, capillary refill, sensation, and motor function.
ddx.acuity_implication: Any deficit requires immediate clinician notification and monitored placement.

teaching_points: A low pain score does not make an open fracture low acuity after analgesia or transfer treatment.
Open fractures require antibiotics, tetanus assessment, wound protection, neurovascular checks, and orthopedic involvement.

inferred_facts.statement: Focused exam should document open wound status, deformity, bleeding, contamination, compartment firmness, pain with passive stretch, distal pulses, capillary refill, motor function, and sensation.
inferred_facts.rationale: The source documents transfer for open tibia/fibula fracture with associated ankle and metatarsal fractures, but it does not provide the structured neurovascular exam.
source_anchors: fracture of the base of the left metatarsal
inferred_facts.expected_action: Place in monitored care, notify clinician and orthopedics, protect the wound, assess tetanus and antibiotics, and repeat neurovascular checks.
inferred_facts.practice_rule: For open long-bone fracture, treat the open fracture and neurovascular exam as the acuity anchor, not the current pain score alone.

history: A 22-year-old white female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of right foot pain, rated as 6 out of 10. She arrived at the ED by walking in. Vital signs are blood pressure 148/86, heart rate 81, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99%, and temperature 98.9掳F. The patient reports no additional symptoms.
outcome: A 22-year-old white female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of right foot pain, rated as 6 out of 10. She arrived at the ED by walking in. augmentation.likely_working_diagnosis: Right foot pain, likely minor musculoskeletal injury pending focused exam

ddx.diagnosis: Foot sprain, contusion, or occult fracture
ddx.support: Walk-in presentation, isolated right foot pain, pain 6/10, one exam or imaging resource, and home disposition support a stable musculoskeletal complaint.
ddx.against_or_missing: No mechanism, swelling, deformity, focal tenderness, or weight-bearing status is documented.
ddx.next_discriminator: Ottawa foot criteria, focal bony tenderness, swelling, weight-bearing ability, and radiograph if indicated.
ddx.acuity_implication: ESI 4 is consistent with one expected resource when neurovascular status is intact.

teaching_points: A benign-appearing foot complaint still needs a focused exam before deciding it is minor.
The source supports low acuity only if the exam does not show deformity, open wound, neurovascular deficit, or inability to bear weight.

inferred_facts.statement: Focused exam should document swelling, bruising, deformity, focal bony tenderness at the midfoot or fifth metatarsal, ability to bear weight, dorsalis pedis pulse, capillary refill, motor function, and sensation.
inferred_facts.rationale: The source documents isolated right foot pain, pain 6/10, one exam or imaging resource, stable vital signs, and home disposition, but no structured foot exam.
inferred_facts.expected_action: Use focused foot exam and imaging criteria to decide whether x-ray, analgesia, splinting, or outpatient follow-up is appropriate.
inferred_facts.practice_rule: For isolated foot pain, document weight-bearing status, focal bony tenderness, and distal neurovascular status before assigning low acuity.

history: A 34-year-old Black/African American male with a history of fistulizing Crohn's disease presented to the ED with severe anal pain. The patient reports increasing anal pain, decreased oral intake due to fear of bowel movements, and an 18-pound weight loss over the last two months. The patient also experiences purulent and bloody discharge from abscesses, episodic fevers, and night sweats. Initial vital signs in the ED were stable.
outcome: A 34-year-old Black/African American male with a history of fistulizing Crohn's disease presented to the ED with severe anal pain. The patient reports increasing anal pain, decreased oral intake due to fear of bowel movements, and an 18-pound weight loss over the last two months. The patient also experiences purulent and bloody discharge from abscesses, episodic fevers, and night sweats. Initial vital signs in the ED were stable.
